subject: Statistics
title: Billy Beane and the Oakland A's
description: A 60-second lesson on Billy Beane and the Oakland A's.

hook.text: In 2002, the Oakland A's had the lowest payroll in baseball — and won 20 games in a row.

buildup.text: General manager Billy Beane used statistics to find undervalued players that scouts overlooked.

discovery.text: On-base percentage predicted wins better than batting average. The market had been pricing the wrong stat.

twist.text: Beane didn't invent the stats — Bill James did in the 1970s. But Beane was the first to bet a team on them.

climax.text: Moneyball proved that data could compete with money. Every major sport adopted analytics within a decade.

punchline.text: The cheapest team won because they measured what mattered.

quiz.question: What was the key insight behind Moneyball?
options: On-base percentage predicted wins better than batting average — the market mispriced player value
Paying players more money always leads to more wins
Older players are always more valuable than younger ones
